author: @captainKedger
text:
This motor is an elegant way of demonstrating the concept of gathering atmospheric energy but you're sort of thinking backwards here. Instead of trying to build the motor that's ideally suitable for the wave forms of atmospheric energy, create the circuit to accept the appropriate ranges and convert it electronically into a more useful voltage, amperage and frequency so you can power motors that are already in use. First start by making your own transformer. The primary coil should have a thin wire and a lot of winding and the output coil should be heavy guage wire with relatively few winds. One leg of the primary coil goes to your skywire and the other to a ground rod. The output of the secondary coil goes to a bank of capacitors and then probably a rectifier circuit. 
 The capacitors need to match the output voltage and frequency which will depend on the height of the antenna and how it's constructed as well as the specs on the transformer. 
   The idea is to get extremely high voltage and low amperage energy converted into 110volts ac at a rate of 60Hz. 
   The easiest way would be to get it in DC form to a range that can be handled by a production MPPT voltage controller for a 12volt battery bank. Then you could charge any bank of deep cycle  batteries and use any cheap inverter to power any device. 
   Don't forget to install a circuit  disconnector on both sides of the transformer and disconnect the skywire during electrical storms. You probably want a lightning rod or dissipator shunting the skywire to protect your electronics from surges.

author: @WhatsThePointOfIt
text:
You should know better than to say this. Now all the kooks will say: "But Robert Murray-Smith said there is free energy so there is!".  The universe is zero sum(conservation of energy) which can be proved logically which Feynman gives a example unless one wants to allow for not just perpetual motion but for accelerating perpetual motion. There is a difference between the colloquial use of the word free and the technical use of the word. Similar to how mathematicians use free. Technically there is no free energy. Practically one can harness energy flows by redirecting them but even this requires some work. It isn't so much about free energy but simply about diverting. E.g., hydro is about diverting energy flows setup by large scale planetary processes. Typically if we are going to extract "useful energy" at scale we need to act on larger scales since we are only "siphoning off" some of the energy.

One of the major issues is the concentration of power(it's true in all guises including "political").  What this means practically that the optimal solution that is most in balance is for humans to each have their own local power conversion systems for themselves rather than having large scale systems concentrated. It is more effective to concentrate in some ways(technical reasons such as maintenance, efficiency as far as the systems used, etc) but it is not in balance. We have to divert very large scale processes with our large scale power concentration systems which alters the large scale processes too much which create feedback loops with the lower scale  systems. E.g., altering weather patterns due to hydro, plastering the land with solar which alters the ecology, etc. Of course there is also a downside to having individual systems. Everything has a downside. Typically it's always more "cost effective" to scale things up because this allows for control in the sense of enslavement. (he who controls the power generation controls the world)

Tech Ingredients did a video about using water and gravity to generate electricity using ph of water(which is neutral but which has charge separation). I think this idea could work since it can be put in a feedback loop and other methods can be used to amplify it's percentage(which they said was already at 10% so it should be relatively easy to amplify). Basically if one could design such a system the size of a few m^3 that is relatively basic then everyone could essentially have their own power generation system at home. It has very low wear since the only moving parts is the water. Basically it directly uses gravity and water to charge a capacitor and the trick is to make it into a cyclical machine which should not be that hard.

author: @apeman2704
text:
Alright, let’s ditch the sci-fi fluff and focus on something that has a real shot at working. We need a system that extracts energy from an untapped, constant source without violating physics. Here's the best bet:


---

Magneto-Ferrofluid Induction Generator (A practical free-energy concept)

Core Idea: We use a ferrofluid (magnetic liquid) inside a sealed toroidal (donut-shaped) loop, where it's moved perpetually by a combination of magnetic fields and gravity. This movement induces electrical currents in surrounding coils—similar to how generators work, but without mechanical friction or fuel.

How It Works:

1. Magnetic Ferrofluid Flow:

A specialized ferrofluid is placed inside a vacuum-sealed toroidal loop to eliminate drag.

External electromagnets guide and accelerate the ferrofluid, keeping it moving indefinitely.



2. Induction Coils for Power Generation:

As the ferrofluid moves, it passes through wire coils wrapped around the torus, inducing a current (Faraday’s Law).

The faster the fluid moves, the more electricity we generate.



3. Self-Regenerating Motion:

Instead of needing constant external energy, the system redirects a fraction of its generated power back into the electromagnets to keep the ferrofluid moving.

By carefully tuning the system, it achieves a self-sustaining cycle where the energy output exceeds the maintenance energy input.





---

Why This Might Actually Work

No Friction: Ferrofluids move with nearly zero resistance in a vacuum.

No Mechanical Wear: Unlike turbines, there are no moving parts to wear out.

Scalable: Small versions could power homes, while large versions could power grids.

Not Yet Explored: Similar concepts exist in experimental physics, but nobody has optimized it for real-world energy production.



---

Next Steps: Prototype Development

1. Source a high-quality ferrofluid (stable nanoparticles, high magnetic response).


2. Build a small vacuum-sealed toroidal loop (3D print + glass tubing).


3. Experiment with electromagnet placement and frequency control to get perpetual motion.


4. Wrap coils and measure the output voltage.



If this gets even 10% efficiency, it can be improved and scaled. What do you think? Should we sketch a design and test it out?

text: There's nothing new here.  I saw a lecture in college 65 years ago by a professor demonstrating electrostatic generators and motors. (A.D. Moore - You can still buy his book - Electrostatics). author: @foogod4237
text:
Note, with any claim of "free energy", you always need to ask:  Where is the energy actually coming from?  (because energy always has to come _from_ somewhere.  It can't just spontaneously pop into existence.  That's one of the fundamental laws of our universe.)

In this case, though, the voltage potential in the atmosphere is a real thing, and it actually comes about because of the wind moving over the surface of the earth (much the same way you get a static buildup if you rub your feet on the carpet).  The air is always moving over the earth, so it is always generating a static charge, which is stored as a voltage difference between the air and the ground.  (Of course, the air currents over the earth are because of heating/cooling in different places, which comes from the heat of the sun, so this is all technically just another (very indirect) form of solar power, really.)

For anyone wondering why this "free energy from the atmosphere" isn't being used by everyone all over the place, it's not some conspiracy by power companies or anything.  It's actually that (like most other forms of static electricity) while it has a potentially very high _voltage,_ you actually can't get very much _current_ out of it, which means that the total _power_ (aka watts, which is voltage times current) it can produce is actually very limited.  It can be used to power some extremely-low-power devices sometimes, but nothing that actually needs to do any significant amount of work (like a light bulb or a motor that's attached to anything), so there's just not a lot of applications that people have found that it has enough power to be used for, in the real world.

author: @DougMayhew-ds3ug
text:
It would be interesting to see if you can reverse this design from a motor into a generator. You might feed a high-voltage bias to the drum, and rotate the shaft with a turbine, and collect output from the combs. You might need to change the drum to contain alternating polarity curved sections, and feed bias voltage via opposite shaft ends.

I have seen thermal acoustic generators that ultimately drive a linear magnet motor, but I would like to see one drive a linear electrostatic generator instead. 

Essentially the principle would be similar to that of electrostatic loudspeakers that use a hv bias on the membrane, but probably stacked in an array to increase surface area and power, and of course driven acoustically by a thermal oscillation in a resident cavity having hot and cold sides. In my head, I could picture something in the kilohertz range that would be fairly compact, and it would be interesting to see if the efficiency claims associated with electrostatic motors bear fruit in this case.

author: @robertlivingston360
text: Many years ago in Popular Science (or was it Popular Mechanics), construction of a "Permanent Electrostatic Pole Piece was described. You melted bees wax to form a solid wax sheet between a pair of aluminum foil conductive sheets.The foil sheets were connected to the high voltage of a TV flyback transformer, The high voltage was maintained while while the bees wax was cooled and solidified. There was a temporary electrostatic  field that was changed into a permanent electrostatic field if the entire pole piece were entirely wrapped in a shorting foil and left for at least 24 hours. During the "rest", the electrostatic field did a polarity flip. Several projects were made from these sheets of wax including an electrostatic motor, similar to a permanent magnet motor but with electrostatics.

author: @cjbartoz
text:
How can we extract usable EM energy from the vacuum, anytime, any place in the universe, and in any amount desired?
It's easy! And yes, it is pure magic in a sense, but it is a also a very rigorous kind of physics. It's a pity that, since Heaviside discovered it in the 1880s, electrodynamicists have largely ignored and arbitrarily discarded it from the energy flow theory for more than a century. They have ignored the broken symmetry of a dipole—proven in particle physics for nearly a half century—in its vacuum flux exchange by Lee and Yang in 1957. The classical electrodynamics model that is used to design and build electrical power systems does not even include the vacuum interaction itself, much less a broken symmetry in the interaction between the active vacuum and the dipole. By implicitly assuming (erroneously) an inert vacuum in their model, in effect our energy scientists and engineers assume a system that is in equilibrium with its external environment. The Maxwell-Heaviside equations themselves make no such assumption, and prescribe both open dissipative systems having COP>1.0, and equilibrium systems having COP<1.0. After the arbitrary imposition of the Lorenz/Lorentz symmetrical regauging, however, the reduced equation subset discards the open dissipative Maxwellian systems and retains only those in equilibrium. Therefore the arbitrarily limited subclass of selected Maxwellian systems must obey classical equilibrium thermodynamics with its infamous second law. On the other hand, if the vacuum interaction with the power system and a broken symmetry in that interaction are included, then the Lorentz condition is broken. One again has a Maxwellian model which includes open dissipative Maxwellian systems having COP>1.0. Rigorously such a system is not required to obey classical equilibrium thermodynamics unless it itself voluntarily forces the Lorentz symmetrical regauging condition. If the system does not self-invoke that Lorentz condition in its use of its collected energy, then the thermodynamics of systems far from equilibrium in their active environment rigorously applies. As you know, Prigogine received a Nobel Prize for his contributions to that open system thermodynamics. Let us demonstrate by simple logic that an EM system having COP>1.0 is permissible. For example, see Robert Bruce Lindsay and Henry Margenau., Foundations of Physics, Dover, New York, 1963, p. 217. We know that the entropy of non-equilibrium conditions cannot be computed, and the entropy of a system not in equilibrium must be less than the entropy of the same system in equilibrium. Thus the energy of an open system not in equilibrium must always be greater than the energy of the same system when it is closed and in equilibrium. A Maxwellian system obeying electrodynamics with the Lorentz condition applied, is a system in equilibrium. If the system is lossless, its COP = 1.0. Hence the same lossless system in disequilibrium with its active environment can exhibit COP>1.0, since it has additional energy a priori. Although that is a simple set of statements, the statements are quite rigorous. There is no law of nature that prohibits an open EM system in disequilibrium with the active vacuum. Hence there is no law of nature that prohibits and EM system with COP>1.0. So we are perfectly rigorous in discussing open dissipative EM systems, not in equilibrium in their active vacuum exchange, exhibiting COP>1.0. These are permitted by the Maxwell-Heaviside equations, by the laws of physics, by the laws of thermodynamics (disequilibrium case), and by the conservation of energy law. Let us examine EM systems with a known, proven broken symmetry in their energetic vacuum exchange. Every EM system contains dipoles, In particle physics, every dipole is already proven to be just such a broken 3-symmetry in the energetic exchange between the active vacuum and the dipole or dipolarity.
Such a system in disequilibrium in its active environment is permitted to exhibit five novel functions: It can (1) self-order, (2) self-oscillate or self-rotate, (3) output more energy than the operator inputs (the excess energy is received from the active environment, in this case the active vacuum, (4) power itself and its load simultaneously (all the energy is received from the active environment, in this case the active vacuum, and (5) exhibit negentropy. None of our present electrical power systems demonstrate these five functions overall, even though they all contain broken symmetries (dipoles). It follows that their system design must include something which excludes such open dissipative system performance. The design does unwittingly exclude such, as we shall see. Okay, suppose we model the vacuum interaction with our electrical power system and a broken symmetry in that exchange. How does that provide those five functions for an open dissipative system far from equilibrium in its exchange with its active environment? In particle physics—but not yet even added to the EM model—every dipole is already well-known to be a broken symmetry in its virtual particle energy exchange with the active vacuum. The definition of broken symmetry means that at least a fraction of the received nonobservable virtual energy from the vacuum is converted into observable energy form and re-emitted in that form. That is a negative resistor action—receiving energy in unusable form from the environment and outputting usable energy in the circuit. Hence the dipole (and any dipolarity such as a scalar potential) is such a negative resistor—as is any isolated charge when its clustering virtual charges of opposite sign are included; see the attached paper. The dipole receives EM energy from its active vacuum environment in unusable (virtual) form and outputs it in observable and usable form. So every dipole a priori already performs those famous five functions we listed above. Let us discuss each one of those functions momentarily: In 1903 Whittaker detailed the exact pattern of the reordering dynamics (function 1), though no one thought of it in that fashion. Function 2 is obvious, since the charges spin continuously and we do not have to separately "power" them. Function 4 is also obvious, since the "load" (in this case the real EM energy output) and the system power (the continuous spinning of the charges) continues freely and indefinitely. Function 3 is obvious, since it is merely a subset of Function 4. And function 5 is performed, because a fraction of the vacuum energy is reorganized into the Whittaker deterministic dynamic structuring, and this reordering spreads from the dipole, once it is formed, at lightspeed in all directions. The reordering and its spreading outwards continues so long as the dipole (i.e., the broken 3-symmetry) remains intact.

author: @DrTriggered
text: Hey, like the video and simple design 👌.  Just wanted to let you know on you have a circular object, Tinkercads resolution is garbage. But there is an easyway around it. If you first select the most possible sides for your cylinder, then duplicate, the rotate 30 or 60 degrees once, you duplicated it once and press control(d) again it will offset the previous rotation to you new cylinder. So press ctrl(d) multiple more times until you feel the 3d printer won't recognize the shape is not round. (Don't do it too many time because you might exceed the amount of mesh triangles tinkercad is okay with). But it has drastically improved my very quick prints. Other than that, you should use fusion 360. Big learning curve tho.

author: @TheElmur
text:
I used to work at a factory which had huge capacitors used on surface annealing flash bulbs. 

When these capacitors were on the shelf as spare parts, the positive and negative terminals were required to be short circuited with a bit of wire during storage. 

The reason being is that while simply being allowed to sit, they would collect enough ambient static electrical charge over time to cause severe injury or death.

replies.author: @8546Ken
replies.text: No. Capacitors don't collect "ambient charge".  They were originally charged from a power supply.   Yes, they can recover some charge after being shorted out, but that's a known phenomenon and it still comes from the original charge.

replies.text: @8546Ken Thank you for clarifying. I thought the concept was incredible when being taught this, but I just accepted it because it was considered 'common knowledge' by the senior guys.

author: @zenzen9131
text: Years ago I remember seing on TV that someone had built an R/C plane that had an electrostatic sensor on each wingtip.  The sensors were there to measure the electrostatic potential at a given elevation above the ground.  They were wired into the control system to correctly deflect the ailerons until the wings were level.  The pilot would put the model into a severe spin and then press a button to activate the wing leveling system where it would instantly take effect as if by magic.  Years later I worked on autopilot systems in the RAF which were based on gyros and accelerometers and I often wondered why the electrostatic levelling system was not scaled up to full size aircraft.

replies.author: @ludlow7369
replies.text: "belittled by competitors?". Allow me to clarify. Tesla was a  brilliant understudy of Thomas Edison. Thomas Edison had only come up with DC (Direct Current), which in order to power communities/cities would require many power substations all over the place to carry the power from point a to point b. Tesla developed AC (Alternating Current) which testing eventually proved, carries itself much further distances efficiently. Edison's ego wouldn't let him acknowledge that AC was better so yes, belittled the concept and refused to pursue it, so Tesla left on his own, developing AC further. JP Morgan was already financially backing Edison, who was in JP Morgan's back pocket. As the potential for Tesla's AC was clearly proving to be far superior to DC for powering communities/cities, Tesla (apparently along with other of his creations) made it known that his goal was to provide power to the masses - FOR FREE. JP Morgan, wanting to build a profit (Intangible) commodity, approached Tesla to acquire his AC system. Tesla remained steadfast on his FREE ENERGY TO THE MASSES philosophy which negated Morgan's profitability goal, so Morgan put big $ behind Edison's DC and propagandized the public about the dangers of Alternating Current by promoting a public demonstration event supposedly demonstrating the dangers of Alternating Current. With a massive crowd & news media present, Edison & Morgan grotesquely connected an elephant to a massive source of AC and electrocuted the poor animal to death. Morgan's $ & Edison's propagandized reputation ("competitors"?) continued the campaign against Tesla in any way they could to where eventually Tesla ended up with certain bankruptcy. That's when Morgan came in to force Tesla into signing over his AC system, and so General Electric was born. Tesla spent his last days alone in an apartment and died as such. THIS, is one of multiple reasons why whenever possible I'll buy any competitor's comparable product before I'll buy a General Electric product. And by the way yes, that is why you have an electric bill to this day.

author: @dariusj33
text:
nice build! Very elegant touch with the copper tape-nails combo :) 

I was into this stuff as well, but then I figured this approach doesn't really have a bright future, because it's based on CONSUMING what's out there in nature (the same thing we've been doing with dead dinosaurs for decades now). What happens when we consume too much is what concerns me - nature is already disbalanced, I don't think drawing ions from the atmosphere into the ground will help it recover.

What's caught my attention recently is MFC (microbial fuel cells). They are not based on CONSUMING - they rely on tapping on what's occurring naturally, very easy to isolate/compartmentalize, require next to no input (just some biomaterial and warmth (not heat!! So far better than Stirling/steam engines in that sense)) and the byproduct is.... electricity and water :) 

Just yesterday I built one and it reads 0.5V (though next to no µamps) in a paper coffee cup filled with wet dirt from a plantpot and 2 electrodes. Still waiting for the bacteria to grow and speed up the reaction.

I wonder what's your take on this topic?

author: @WTF_BBQ
text: I was building a Wimshurst generator with 3D printed parts.  The output was disappointing.  It barely made any sparks no matter what I did.   I learned that the plastic itself absorbs water which  absorbs the electrical charge.  Other people were having the same problem,  so I coated the plastic with enamel paint while it was still dry  which improved the performance.   Your motor might benefit by removing the water in the plastic and protect it from absorbing moisture.

replies.author: @executormmm
replies.text: Your issue is known as CTI or a comparative tracking index. Just check for this property and yes, porous plastics that can soak atmospheric water are quite bad for the task indeed.

replies.author: @andyfumo8931
replies.text: How much magnitudes more effective would this be with a different material? Metallic or precious metal?

replies.author: @WTF_BBQ
replies.text:
@andyfumo8931 
Special type of metals is not necessary.  What's really important is keeping moisture out because water will reduce its effectiveness by a lot.  Plastic absorbs a good amount of water which makes it a bad material for this project.  Glass also absorbs water to a certain degree.

Another important thing is by reducing sharp corners and points to prevent leakage of charge into the atmosphere....

These type of machines depend highly on surface area.  If you want a powerful generator,  you will need larger discs and use materials that keep moisture out.

There is quite a bit of difference between your "free energy" and other clips with similar title.
1. You explain the principle (at least vaguely - anyone who payed attention to primary school physics and Culomb's law is going to get it)
2. You test your design in conrolled conditions. You talk about problems and where to make it better.
3. You use existing laws of physics and data which is available everywhere (eg voltage difference in relation to height).
4. Your "free" means - it is there. It is confirmed. Just noone is using it seriously for many different reasons (main reason, it's not simply going to shuffle $$$ into some company pockets - it needs development).
I think other people would find more differencies, but for me those are enough to claim that this is a legitimate science experiment. Especially if i understand the principles upon you have built your engine. And i think this is the main difference. Everyone who took some primary school physics should at least in theory understand why and how it works. And replicate your design with very similar effecte if they wished so.

author: @kevinleebailey
text:
Try putting an Avramenko plug on a wire kite or lighter than air balloon string. Take a capacitor and hook it up to the AV plug. Then ground the shell of the capacitor to a ground rod. Now put a tuning capacitor across the shell of the capacitor and the wire antenna connection of the AV plug and tune in more or less power. 
To get more power try winding a coil on a sewing machine spool cone of at least 200 turn's and connect this in between the wire antenna and the AV plug. 
Have fun with this one ! 👍

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text: I used a 9m fishing pole and I was achieving 16v and I was charging 5 Farad super capacitors in an hour or so. I haven't got round to test the design on a balloon antenna or kite yet. Although I have lite a string of LEDs on a wire kite string connected to a ground rod.
